Authors: Ze Peng; Jian Zhang; Lei Qi; Yang Gao; Yinghuan Shi

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence

DOI: 10.1109/tpami.2026.3691379

Relevance: 0.796 (consider)

The observation that activation sparsity emerges in MLP blocks of standardly trained Transformers offers an opportunity to drastically reduce computation costs without sacrificing performance. To theoretically explain this phenomenon, existing works have shown that activation sparsity does not result from the data properties or data fitting but from the implicit bias of the training process. However, these connections are obtained with strong assumptions (e.g., shallow networks, a small number of training steps, and special training techniques), which cannot be applied to deep models standardly trained with a large number of steps. Different from these works, we find that the flatness of loss landscapes is also closely related to the MLP activation sparsity and can serve as a weaker assumption because it naturally emerges in the standard training of deep networks without the above strong assumptions. Specifically, we find that 1) the MLP activation sparsity equals a ratio between “augmented f latness” (a weighted sum of flatness measures) and the product of the input norm and activation gradient of the MLP. We empirically find that this ratio decreases during training, leading to sparse activations. 2) We also propose the notion of derivative sparsity, which reduces to activation sparsity under ReLU, but further enables pruning in the backward propagation and is more stable than activation sparsity. With the theoretical findings, we can further encourage activation sparsity by decreasing the numerator and increasing the denominator of the ratio: 1) To improve (lower) the flatness, we add different bias vectors to input tokens of MLP blocks to strengthen stochastic gradient noises that drive the model to a flat area. 2) We restrict the lower bound of affine parameters in LayerNorm to increase the input norm of MLPs. 3) To increase the activation sparsity, we propose an activation function JSReLU to encourage the search of parameters with sparse derivatives and sparse activations. These plug-and-play modifications can effectively reduce the ratio and produce sparser activations. Experiments on ImageNet-1K and C4 demonstrate relative improvements of at least 36% on inference sparsity and at least 50% on training sparsity over vanilla Transformers, indicating further potential cost reduction in both inference and training.

item.title: Hyperspectral image classification via efficient global spectral supertoken clustering
item.dc:creator: Peifu Liu
Tingfa Xu
Jie Wang
Huan Chen
Huiyan Bai
Jianan Li
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Hyperspectral image classification demands spatially coherent predictions and precise boundary delineation. Yet prevailing superpixel-based methods face an inherent contradiction: clustering aggregates similar pixels into regions, but the subsequent classifier operates pixel-wise, undermining regional consistency. Consequently, existing approaches do not guarantee region-level, boundary-aligned classification. To address this limitation, we propose the Dual-stage Spectrum-Constrained Clustering-based Classifier (DSCC), an end-to-end framework that explicitly decouples clustering from classification by first grouping spectral similar and spatially proximate pixels into spectral supertokens and then performing token-level prediction. At its core, DSCC computes an image-level multi-criteria feature distance between pixels and centers, followed by a locality-aware assignment regularization, enabling the generation of boundary-preserving spectral supertokens. A density-isolation based center selection further yields representative, well-separated centers, reducing redundancy and improving robustness to scale variation. To accommodate mixed land-cover compositions within each token, we introduce a soft-label scheme that encodes class proportions and improves robustness for mixed-class tokens. DSCC attains a CF1 of 0.728 at 197.75 FPS on the WHU-OHS dataset, offering a superior accuracy–efficiency trade-off compared with state-of-the-art methods. Extensive experiments further validate the effectiveness and generality of the proposed dual-stage paradigm for hyperspectral image classification. The source code is available at https://github.com/laprf/DSCC . Published: 2026-05-07T17:35:36+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.790 (consider)

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.04.041

Relevance: 0.790 (consider)

Hyperspectral image classification demands spatially coherent predictions and precise boundary delineation. Yet prevailing superpixel-based methods face an inherent contradiction: clustering aggregates similar pixels into regions, but the subsequent classifier operates pixel-wise, undermining regional consistency. Consequently, existing approaches do not guarantee region-level, boundary-aligned classification. To address this limitation, we propose the Dual-stage Spectrum-Constrained Clustering-based Classifier (DSCC), an end-to-end framework that explicitly decouples clustering from classification by first grouping spectral similar and spatially proximate pixels into spectral supertokens and then performing token-level prediction. At its core, DSCC computes an image-level multi-criteria feature distance between pixels and centers, followed by a locality-aware assignment regularization, enabling the generation of boundary-preserving spectral supertokens. A density-isolation based center selection further yields representative, well-separated centers, reducing redundancy and improving robustness to scale variation. To accommodate mixed land-cover compositions within each token, we introduce a soft-label scheme that encodes class proportions and improves robustness for mixed-class tokens. DSCC attains a CF1 of 0.728 at 197.75 FPS on the WHU-OHS dataset, offering a superior accuracy–efficiency trade-off compared with state-of-the-art methods. Extensive experiments further validate the effectiveness and generality of the proposed dual-stage paradigm for hyperspectral image classification. The source code is available at https://github.com/laprf/DSCC .

item.title: DQA-DETR: Dynamic Query Aggregation for Oriented Object Detection in Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Yongchen Yao
Songwei Pei
Yuanzhou Huang
Qian Li
Shangguang Wang
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
item.description: Oriented object detection in remote sensing images faces challenges, such as arbitrary object orientations and uneven distribution of object quantities. Although detection transformer (DETR)-based methods have recently achieved promising progress in remote sensing object detection, they still face challenges when using a fixed query budget in scenes where object quantities vary significantly due to large-scale imaging and complex spatial distributions. A fixed query budget can be poorly matched to such variability, producing redundant queries in sparse scenes and many high-quality negatives under one-to-one matching, which may hinder optimization efficiency and degrade performance. To address this issue, we propose a transformer-based dynamic query aggregation detection framework (DQA-DETR) to alleviate query redundancy in remote sensing scenarios. DQA-DETR incorporates three key modules: aggregation center predictor, aggregation center selector, and query aggregator, which are responsible for predicting the required number of representative queries, selecting high-quality aggregation centers, and aggregating semantically related queries via a multihead attention mechanism, respectively. This strategy adaptively adjusts the number of queries involved in matching according to the object quantity in each image, effectively reducing redundancy and enhancing the model’s adaptability in both sparse and dense scenes. Extensive experiments on the DOTA v1.0 and v1.5 datasets demonstrate that DQA-DETR improves detection performance, while maintaining the end-to-end advantages of DETR, exhibiting strong competitiveness among state-of-the-art methods. Published: 2026-05-07T19:53:38+00:00 Venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing Score: 0.790 (consider)

Oriented object detection in remote sensing images faces challenges, such as arbitrary object orientations and uneven distribution of object quantities. Although detection transformer (DETR)-based methods have recently achieved promising progress in remote sensing object detection, they still face challenges when using a fixed query budget in scenes where object quantities vary significantly due to large-scale imaging and complex spatial distributions. A fixed query budget can be poorly matched to such variability, producing redundant queries in sparse scenes and many high-quality negatives under one-to-one matching, which may hinder optimization efficiency and degrade performance. To address this issue, we propose a transformer-based dynamic query aggregation detection framework (DQA-DETR) to alleviate query redundancy in remote sensing scenarios. DQA-DETR incorporates three key modules: aggregation center predictor, aggregation center selector, and query aggregator, which are responsible for predicting the required number of representative queries, selecting high-quality aggregation centers, and aggregating semantically related queries via a multihead attention mechanism, respectively. This strategy adaptively adjusts the number of queries involved in matching according to the object quantity in each image, effectively reducing redundancy and enhancing the model’s adaptability in both sparse and dense scenes. Extensive experiments on the DOTA v1.0 and v1.5 datasets demonstrate that DQA-DETR improves detection performance, while maintaining the end-to-end advantages of DETR, exhibiting strong competitiveness among state-of-the-art methods.

item.title: MrsNet: Multi-scale dual-domain reconstruction with spatial-spectral masked network for hyperspectral anomaly detection
item.dc:creator: Zhe Zhao
Jiangluqi Song
Huixin Zhou
Yong Zhu
item.prism:publicationName: Pattern Recognition
item.description: In recent years, reconstruction-based methods have achieved notable success in hyperspectral anomaly detection (HAD). However, accurately recovering high-quality background remains challenging due to background complexity and interference from anomalous pixels. Furthermore, spectral variations and underutilized frequency-domain information often limit detection performance. To address these issues, this paper proposes a multi-scale dual-domain reconstruction network with a spatial–spectral masking strategy for HAD, named MrsNet. Specifically, a spatial–spectral masking (SSM) module is first introduced to reduce the influence of anomalies and spectral variability during feature extraction. A multi-scale dual-domain feature interaction module (DFIM) is then designed to model complex backgrounds by extracting and integrating multi-scale features from both spatial and frequency domains. Specifically, multi-scale convolution blocks are used to extract spatial features, and multi-scale modified wavelet convolution is employed to extract features in the frequency domain. Additionally, feature interaction is used to fully integrate features from different domains. To compensate for potential background loss caused by masking, a smoothness prior-guided module (SPM) leverages local homogeneity to assist the decoder in background reconstruction. Anomalies are finally identified by computing the Mahalanobis distance between the input HSI and the reconstructed background. Experimental results on four hyperspectral datasets and two UAV datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of MrsNet compared with nine state-of-the-art approaches. Published: 2026-05-07T15:59:21+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.789 (consider)

Authors: Zhe Zhao; Jiangluqi Song; Huixin Zhou; Yong Zhu

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113925

Relevance: 0.789 (consider)

In recent years, reconstruction-based methods have achieved notable success in hyperspectral anomaly detection (HAD). However, accurately recovering high-quality background remains challenging due to background complexity and interference from anomalous pixels. Furthermore, spectral variations and underutilized frequency-domain information often limit detection performance. To address these issues, this paper proposes a multi-scale dual-domain reconstruction network with a spatial–spectral masking strategy for HAD, named MrsNet. Specifically, a spatial–spectral masking (SSM) module is first introduced to reduce the influence of anomalies and spectral variability during feature extraction. A multi-scale dual-domain feature interaction module (DFIM) is then designed to model complex backgrounds by extracting and integrating multi-scale features from both spatial and frequency domains. Specifically, multi-scale convolution blocks are used to extract spatial features, and multi-scale modified wavelet convolution is employed to extract features in the frequency domain. Additionally, feature interaction is used to fully integrate features from different domains. To compensate for potential background loss caused by masking, a smoothness prior-guided module (SPM) leverages local homogeneity to assist the decoder in background reconstruction. Anomalies are finally identified by computing the Mahalanobis distance between the input HSI and the reconstructed background. Experimental results on four hyperspectral datasets and two UAV datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of MrsNet compared with nine state-of-the-art approaches.

item.title: Scattering-Clutter Disentangled Prototypes for SAR Open Set Recognition
item.dc:creator: Xuxiang He
Zheyuan Cai
Jingming Sun
Jieyuan Yang
Qiang Cheng
Yue Huang
Xinghao Ding
Yuhao Yang
Xiaotong Tu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) systems face significant challenges in Open Set Recognition (OSR) due to the complex coupling between target scattering and background clutter, as well as the severe appearance variations induced by aspect changes, inevitably leading to ambiguous decision boundaries in OSR. To address these issues, we propose a Scattering-Clutter Disentangled Prototypes Network (SCDP-Net). First, we design a dual-stream architecture with physics-aware preprocessing to separate scattering-dominant representations from clutter-related representations. Second, to handle aspect-dependent variations, we introduce a multi-view prototype learning mechanism that models the diverse scattering patterns of targets within a compact feature space. Furthermore, a test-time adaptation strategy is developed to dynamically calibrate the decision boundary for unknown classes during inference. Experiments on the large-scale ATRNet-STAR dataset demonstrate that SCDP-Net outperforms state-of-the-art methods, improving AUROC by 5.27% and 3.26% under standard and extended operating conditions, respectively. Published: 2026-05-07T19:56:24+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.788 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xuxiang He; Zheyuan Cai; Jingming Sun; Jieyuan Yang; Qiang Cheng; Yue Huang; Xinghao Ding; Yuhao Yang; Xiaotong Tu

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3691105

Relevance: 0.788 (consider)

Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) systems face significant challenges in Open Set Recognition (OSR) due to the complex coupling between target scattering and background clutter, as well as the severe appearance variations induced by aspect changes, inevitably leading to ambiguous decision boundaries in OSR. To address these issues, we propose a Scattering-Clutter Disentangled Prototypes Network (SCDP-Net). First, we design a dual-stream architecture with physics-aware preprocessing to separate scattering-dominant representations from clutter-related representations. Second, to handle aspect-dependent variations, we introduce a multi-view prototype learning mechanism that models the diverse scattering patterns of targets within a compact feature space. Furthermore, a test-time adaptation strategy is developed to dynamically calibrate the decision boundary for unknown classes during inference. Experiments on the large-scale ATRNet-STAR dataset demonstrate that SCDP-Net outperforms state-of-the-art methods, improving AUROC by 5.27% and 3.26% under standard and extended operating conditions, respectively.

item.title: A Semantically-Guided Optimization Framework for Few-Shot HRRP Recognition via Vision-Language Priors
item.dc:creator: Lingfeng Chen
Panhe Hu
Shuanghui Zhang
Xiangfeng Qiu
Zhen Liu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
item.description: Few-shot Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) based on High-Resolution Range Profiles (HRRP) is crucial for non cooperative scenarios, as high-quality labeled data is scarce in practical application. Since projecting 3D targets onto 1D radar echoes inevitably introduces information loss, the feature distribution in HRRP becomes highly complex, making it difficult to learn a generalized recognition model with limited samples. In this work, a Semantically-Guided Optimization Framework (SGOF) is proposed to address this challenge by incorporating external knowledge priors. Specifically, the few-shot ATR task is first converted into a network parameter optimization process guided by a frozen Vision Language Model (VLM). Subsequently, a semantic regularized update strategy is further introduced to ensure discriminative feature extraction, by proposing the Physics-Aware Semantic Prior Generation (PSPG) and Dual-Constrained Manifold Optimization (DCMO) modules. In PSPG, Large Language Models (LLMs) are prompted to generate physics-aware semantic anchors that encode explicit electromagnetic scattering and geometric properties, regularizing the optimization landscape in DCMO. Within the DCMO framework, the Manifold Adaptation Operator (MAO) is then optimized via a dual-objective function. This function balances the semantic consistency term and the discriminative data-fidelity term, which are employed for physical prior alignment and inter-class separability enforcement, respectively. Finally, extensive simulations based on both aircraft electromagnetic simulation and measured datasets are conducted to demonstrate the superior robustness and generalization capabilities of the proposed method. Published: 2026-05-07T19:55:34+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems Score: 0.788 (consider)

Authors: Lingfeng Chen; Panhe Hu; Shuanghui Zhang; Xiangfeng Qiu; Zhen Liu

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems

DOI: 10.1109/taes.2026.3691238

Relevance: 0.788 (consider)

Few-shot Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) based on High-Resolution Range Profiles (HRRP) is crucial for non cooperative scenarios, as high-quality labeled data is scarce in practical application. Since projecting 3D targets onto 1D radar echoes inevitably introduces information loss, the feature distribution in HRRP becomes highly complex, making it difficult to learn a generalized recognition model with limited samples. In this work, a Semantically-Guided Optimization Framework (SGOF) is proposed to address this challenge by incorporating external knowledge priors. Specifically, the few-shot ATR task is first converted into a network parameter optimization process guided by a frozen Vision Language Model (VLM). Subsequently, a semantic regularized update strategy is further introduced to ensure discriminative feature extraction, by proposing the Physics-Aware Semantic Prior Generation (PSPG) and Dual-Constrained Manifold Optimization (DCMO) modules. In PSPG, Large Language Models (LLMs) are prompted to generate physics-aware semantic anchors that encode explicit electromagnetic scattering and geometric properties, regularizing the optimization landscape in DCMO. Within the DCMO framework, the Manifold Adaptation Operator (MAO) is then optimized via a dual-objective function. This function balances the semantic consistency term and the discriminative data-fidelity term, which are employed for physical prior alignment and inter-class separability enforcement, respectively. Finally, extensive simulations based on both aircraft electromagnetic simulation and measured datasets are conducted to demonstrate the superior robustness and generalization capabilities of the proposed method.

item.title: Robust Remote Sensing Scene Classification via Diffusion-Based Knowledge Distillation under Mixed-Noise Degradation
item.dc:creator: Longwei Li
Xinyu Chu
Xiang Li
Nan Li
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: High-resolution remote sensing (RS) scene classification is pivotal for intelligent earth observation but faces significant deployment challenges on resource-constrained devices, primarily due to the prohibitive computational cost of state-of-the-art deep models. Knowledge distillation (KD) mitigates this by transferring knowledge from a large teacher model to a compact student model, yet its efficacy is often limited by representational capacity gap between models and further compromised by the complex, mixed-noise degradation inherent in RS imagery during acquisition and transmission. To address this, we adapt and enhance a diffusion-based KD framework (DiffKD) for the RS domain. Our approach explicitly purifies noisy student features using a diffusion model trained on clean teacher features, thereby facilitating more purified knowledge transfer. We further design a comprehensive hybrid noise model that simulates realistic RS physical degradations (sensor, transmission, compression, and atmospheric noise) for rigorous robustness evaluation. A novel teacher model optimization strategy combining progressive fine-tuning and layer freezing is proposed to bridge the domain gap from natural to RS images and ensure a high-quality knowledge source. Extensive experiments on four public RS scene classification benchmarks (NWPU-RESISC45, AID, EuroSAT, PatternNet) demonstrate that our adapted DiffKD framework consistently outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods in classification accuracy. Crucially, it exhibits exceptional robustness under varying noise intensities, with the student model even surpassing the teacher's performance. This work provides a practical solution for deploying high-performance, noise-resilient scene classification models on edge devices. Code is available at https://github.com/maomaoxy/DiffKD_2025. Published: 2026-05-07T19:53:06+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.785 (consider)

High-resolution remote sensing (RS) scene classification is pivotal for intelligent earth observation but faces significant deployment challenges on resource-constrained devices, primarily due to the prohibitive computational cost of state-of-the-art deep models. Knowledge distillation (KD) mitigates this by transferring knowledge from a large teacher model to a compact student model, yet its efficacy is often limited by representational capacity gap between models and further compromised by the complex, mixed-noise degradation inherent in RS imagery during acquisition and transmission. To address this, we adapt and enhance a diffusion-based KD framework (DiffKD) for the RS domain. Our approach explicitly purifies noisy student features using a diffusion model trained on clean teacher features, thereby facilitating more purified knowledge transfer. We further design a comprehensive hybrid noise model that simulates realistic RS physical degradations (sensor, transmission, compression, and atmospheric noise) for rigorous robustness evaluation. A novel teacher model optimization strategy combining progressive fine-tuning and layer freezing is proposed to bridge the domain gap from natural to RS images and ensure a high-quality knowledge source. Extensive experiments on four public RS scene classification benchmarks (NWPU-RESISC45, AID, EuroSAT, PatternNet) demonstrate that our adapted DiffKD framework consistently outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods in classification accuracy. Crucially, it exhibits exceptional robustness under varying noise intensities, with the student model even surpassing the teacher's performance. This work provides a practical solution for deploying high-performance, noise-resilient scene classification models on edge devices. Code is available at https://github.com/maomaoxy/DiffKD_2025.

item.title: Local Cross-Channel Interaction Coverage Network for Infrared Small Target Detection
item.dc:creator: Yifan Ji
Ende Wang
Jiageng Liu
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
item.description: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) faces challenges such as low signal-to-noise ratios (SNR), complex backgrounds, and semantic information loss in downsampling, limiting robustness and efficiency of existing methods. To address this issue, a novel model, namely Local Cross-Channel Interaction Coverage Network (LCICNet), is proposed for infrared small target detection by integrating the Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) module with channel-adaptive kernels. Specially, we first introduce lightweight convolutions with adaptive kernels to improve computational efficiency, for which a local contrast method that expands the receptive field and minimizes the loss of high-level semantic information is given. Then, we design a Local Contrast Sensitivity (LCS) loss function, which adjusts Intersection over Union (IoU) loss weights based on target scale. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of LCICNet in addressing challenges in infrared small target detection, achieving accurate infrared small target detection with 0.43M parameters. The source can be found online at https://github.com/Jiyifan15/ LCICNet-for-IRSTD. Published: 2026-05-08T19:43:30+00:00 Venue: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters Score: 0.785 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yifan Ji; Ende Wang; Jiageng Liu

Journal: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters

DOI: 10.1109/lgrs.2026.3691332

Relevance: 0.785 (consider)

Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) faces challenges such as low signal-to-noise ratios (SNR), complex backgrounds, and semantic information loss in downsampling, limiting robustness and efficiency of existing methods. To address this issue, a novel model, namely Local Cross-Channel Interaction Coverage Network (LCICNet), is proposed for infrared small target detection by integrating the Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) module with channel-adaptive kernels. Specially, we first introduce lightweight convolutions with adaptive kernels to improve computational efficiency, for which a local contrast method that expands the receptive field and minimizes the loss of high-level semantic information is given. Then, we design a Local Contrast Sensitivity (LCS) loss function, which adjusts Intersection over Union (IoU) loss weights based on target scale. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of LCICNet in addressing challenges in infrared small target detection, achieving accurate infrared small target detection with 0.43M parameters. The source can be found online at https://github.com/Jiyifan15/ LCICNet-for-IRSTD.

item.title: Weighted sample correlation knowledge distillation for visual recognition
item.dc:creator: Daidai Liu
Fan Gao
Jianping Gou
Jiacheng Liu
Baosheng Yu
Zhang Yi
item.prism:publicationName: Pattern Recognition
item.description: Deploying deep neural networks with massive parameter counts on resource-constrained edge devices remains a significant challenge due to limited computational power, storage capacity, and energy efficiency. Knowledge distillation has emerged as a promising model compression technique, enabling the transfer of knowledge from a large teacher model to a compact student model. Traditional relation-based knowledge distillation methods focus on distilling inter-instance relational knowledge—typically, pairwise similarities between samples—but often overlook the asymmetry in each sample’s contribution to these relations. In reality, the relational information derived from a pair of samples may hold different importance for each sample within the feature space. To address this issue, we propose Weighted Sample Correlation Knowledge Distillation (WSCKD), a novel approach that explicitly models the asymmetric contributions of samples to relational knowledge. WSCKD leverages pairwise similarities in the source (teacher) embedding space as transferable knowledge and introduces two asymmetric loss functions: the unidirectional weighted distillation loss and the bidirectional weighted distillation loss. These losses enable the student model to prioritize more informative relationships during training, without imposing constraints on the manifold structure of the student’s embedding space. Extensive experiments and ablation studies on multiple visual image recognition benchmarks demonstrate that WSCKD outperforms sixteen distillation methods. Published: 2026-05-07T06:53:32+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.784 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Daidai Liu; Fan Gao; Jianping Gou; Jiacheng Liu; Baosheng Yu; Zhang Yi

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113911

Relevance: 0.784 (consider)

Deploying deep neural networks with massive parameter counts on resource-constrained edge devices remains a significant challenge due to limited computational power, storage capacity, and energy efficiency. Knowledge distillation has emerged as a promising model compression technique, enabling the transfer of knowledge from a large teacher model to a compact student model. Traditional relation-based knowledge distillation methods focus on distilling inter-instance relational knowledge—typically, pairwise similarities between samples—but often overlook the asymmetry in each sample’s contribution to these relations. In reality, the relational information derived from a pair of samples may hold different importance for each sample within the feature space. To address this issue, we propose Weighted Sample Correlation Knowledge Distillation (WSCKD), a novel approach that explicitly models the asymmetric contributions of samples to relational knowledge. WSCKD leverages pairwise similarities in the source (teacher) embedding space as transferable knowledge and introduces two asymmetric loss functions: the unidirectional weighted distillation loss and the bidirectional weighted distillation loss. These losses enable the student model to prioritize more informative relationships during training, without imposing constraints on the manifold structure of the student’s embedding space. Extensive experiments and ablation studies on multiple visual image recognition benchmarks demonstrate that WSCKD outperforms sixteen distillation methods.
